name: Health and Nutrition
description: A comprehensive exploration of nutrition science covering macronutrients, micronutrients, hydration, dietary guidelines, sports nutrition, and global food systems for healthy adolescent lifestyles.

modules.title: Introduction to Nutrition
modules.objectives: Define nutrition and its importance to health, identify the six classes of nutrients, and understand how food choices affect overall wellness.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Six Classes of Nutrients Overview
modules.practiceNote: Use the MyPlate plan tool to assess your current dietary pattern
modules.assignment: Keep a 3-day food diary and analyze your intake using the MyPlate framework, identifying nutrients you consume adequately and areas for improvement.

modules.title: Macronutrients: Carbohydrates
modules.titleZh: 宏量营养素：碳水化合物
modules.objectives: Identify types of carbohydrates (simple vs. complex, fiber), understand their role as the body's primary energy source, and evaluate healthy carbohydrate food choices.
modules.readingNote: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health — Carbohydrates
modules.practiceNote: Identify five high-quality carbohydrate sources and five poor-quality sources from your diet
modules.assignment: Create a report comparing simple and complex carbohydrates, including the glycemic index concept, fiber's role in health, and three food swaps for better carbohydrate choices.

modules.title: Macronutrients: Proteins
modules.objectives: Explain the structure and functions of protein, differentiate complete from incomplete proteins, and determine protein needs for different activity levels.
modules.readingNote: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health — The Nutrition Source: Protein
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Protein: What It Is and Why You Need It
modules.practiceNote: Identify your estimated daily protein needs and plan a high-protein meal that includes a complete protein source
modules.assignment: Calculate your recommended daily protein intake based on body weight and activity level, and plan a full day of meals that meets this target with diverse protein sources.

modules.title: Macronutrients: Fats
modules.objectives: Classify dietary fats (saturated, unsaturated, trans), understand their health implications, and identify food sources of healthy fats.
modules.readingNote: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health — Fats and Cholesterol
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Types of Dietary Fat Explained
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Good Fats vs Bad Fats for Health
modules.practiceNote: Analyze a food label for fat content and classify fats found in the product
modules.assignment: Write a guide explaining the three main types of dietary fat, their health effects, food sources for each, and practical advice for choosing heart-healthy fats.

modules.title: Micronutrients: Vitamins
modules.objectives: Identify water-soluble and fat-soluble vitamins, their functions, food sources, and consequences of deficiency or excess.
modules.readingNote: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Vitamins and Minerals
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Key Vitamins and Their Functions
modules.practiceNote: Choose two vitamins and research their functions, deficiency symptoms, and best food sources
modules.assignment: Create a vitamin reference chart for 8 key vitamins (4 fat-soluble, 4 water-soluble) including daily recommended amounts, deficiency symptoms, and top food sources.

modules.title: Micronutrients: Minerals
modules.objectives: Identify key dietary minerals (calcium, iron, potassium, sodium, zinc), their roles in body function, deficiency conditions, and food sources.
modules.readingNote: NIH Office of Dietary Supplements — Minerals
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Essential Minerals and Their Functions
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Calcium, Iron, and Key Minerals Explained
modules.practiceNote: Analyze your typical diet for calcium and iron intake adequacy
modules.assignment: Research three minerals important for adolescent health (calcium, iron, zinc), explaining their functions, recommended daily intake, and consequences of deficiency with dietary solutions.

modules.title: Water & Hydration
modules.objectives: Explain water's roles in the body, understand daily hydration requirements, and recognize signs of dehydration and overhydration.
modules.readingNote: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health — Water
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Importance of Hydration and Water Intake
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Dehydration: Signs and Effects on the Body
modules.practiceNote: Track your fluid intake for 3 days and compare to daily recommended amounts
modules.assignment: Write a hydration guide for teenagers that explains daily water needs, factors that increase hydration needs, signs of dehydration, and tips for staying hydrated.

modules.title: Energy Balance & Weight Management
modules.objectives: Explain the concept of energy balance (calories in vs. out), understand basal metabolic rate, and evaluate healthy approaches to weight management.
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Energy Balance and Weight Management
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Calories In vs Calories Out Explained
modules.practiceNote: Use CDC BMI calculator and healthy weight resources to assess personal weight status
modules.assignment: Research the concept of energy balance and write a one-page essay explaining why crash diets are ineffective and unsustainable compared to lifestyle-based approaches.

modules.title: Eating Patterns & Dietary Guidelines
modules.objectives: Identify current US Dietary Guidelines, evaluate different eating patterns (Mediterranean, DASH, plant-based), and understand how to read and apply MyPlate.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Mediterranean Diet vs Other Eating Patterns
modules.practiceNote: Compare your 3-day food diary to the USDA Dietary Guidelines recommendations
modules.assignment: Compare the Mediterranean and DASH diets, summarizing their key principles, health benefits, and practical tips for adopting elements of each into everyday eating.

modules.title: Sports Nutrition
modules.objectives: Explain how nutritional needs change with physical activity, understand fueling strategies before, during, and after exercise, and evaluate sports supplements critically.
modules.readingNote: ACE Fitness — Sports Nutrition for Athletes
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Pre and Post Workout Nutrition
modules.practiceNote: Plan a pre-workout meal and post-workout recovery snack using sports nutrition principles
modules.assignment: Develop a complete one-day sports nutrition plan for a high school soccer player on a game day, including all meals, snacks, and hydration with rationale for each choice.

modules.title: Food Labels & Consumer Health
modules.titleZh: 食品标签与消费者健康
modules.objectives: Interpret the Nutrition Facts label, understand ingredient lists, identify marketing claims, and make informed food choices as a health-conscious consumer.
modules.readingNote: FDA — How to Understand and Use the Nutrition Facts Label
modules.videoNote: YouTube — How to Read a Nutrition Facts Label
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Food Marketing Tricks to Watch Out For
modules.practiceNote: Analyze and compare nutrition labels from two similar food products
modules.assignment: Compare nutrition labels from three popular teen snack products, evaluating serving sizes, sugar, sodium, and nutrient density, then recommend the healthiest choice with justification.

modules.title: Eating Disorders
modules.objectives: Identify types of eating disorders (anorexia, bulimia, binge eating disorder), understand their causes and health consequences, and know how to seek help.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Signs of Eating Disorders and Getting Help
modules.practiceNote: Review NIMH resources on eating disorder warning signs and treatment options
modules.assignment: Write a one-page resource guide for teens on eating disorders, covering warning signs, physical and emotional consequences, and how to support a friend who may be struggling.

modules.title: Nutrition Through the Lifespan
modules.objectives: Explain how nutritional needs change across the lifespan from adolescence through older adulthood, and identify special dietary considerations for each life stage.
modules.readingNote: USDA MyPlate — Nutrition Across the Lifespan
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Nutrition Needs Across the Lifespan
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Teen Nutrition vs Adult Nutrition
modules.practiceNote: Compare recommended daily servings for a teenager vs. an older adult using MyPlate tools
modules.assignment: Create a comparison chart of nutritional needs and key nutrients for four life stages: adolescent, young adult, middle-aged adult, and older adult, with food source examples.

modules.title: Global Food Systems & Sustainability
modules.titleZh: 全球食品体系与可持续性
modules.objectives: Understand how food is produced and distributed globally, identify environmental impacts of food choices, and explore sustainable and equitable food practices.
modules.readingNote: WHO — Food Safety and Global Food Systems
modules.videoNote: YouTube — Global Food Systems and Sustainability
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Food Waste and the Environment
modules.practiceNote: Explore the World Food Prize website and reflect on global food security challenges
modules.assignment: Write a reflective essay on the environmental impact of your diet, proposing three specific dietary changes you could make to reduce your food's environmental footprint.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the six classes of nutrients?
quizQuestions.explanation: The six classes of nutrients are carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, minerals, and water. Enzymes are biological catalysts, not a nutrient class.

quizQuestions.question: Which nutrient class provides the most calories per gram?
quizQuestions.explanation: Fat provides 9 calories per gram, compared to 4 calories per gram for both carbohydrates and protein.

quizQuestions.question: The USDA MyPlate icon is divided into how many food groups?
quizQuestions.explanation: MyPlate is divided into five food groups: Fruits, Vegetables, Grains, Protein, and Dairy.

quizQuestions.question: Which nutrient class does NOT provide calories?
quizQuestions.explanation: Vitamins and minerals do not provide calories; they support metabolic processes without contributing energy directly.

quizQuestions.question: Good nutrition is important for adolescents primarily because:
options: It supports growth, brain development, energy, and long-term disease prevention
It is less important than sleep
quizQuestions.explanation: Adequate nutrition during adolescence supports rapid physical growth, cognitive development, energy for activity, and establishes lifelong health patterns.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary function of carbohydrates in the body?
options: Providing energy (especially for the brain and during exercise)
quizQuestions.explanation: Carbohydrates are the body's preferred energy source, especially for brain function and moderate-to-high intensity exercise.

quizQuestions.question: Dietary fiber is best described as:
options: An indigestible carbohydrate that supports digestive health
quizQuestions.explanation: Dietary fiber is a complex carbohydrate that humans cannot fully digest, supporting digestive health, satiety, and blood sugar regulation.

quizQuestions.question: The glycemic index measures:
options: The total carbohydrate content of a food
How quickly a food raises blood glucose levels
The amount of sugar in a product
quizQuestions.explanation: The glycemic index ranks foods by how quickly they raise blood sugar; high-GI foods cause rapid spikes, low-GI foods cause gradual rises.

quizQuestions.question: Which food is the best source of complex carbohydrates?
quizQuestions.explanation: Whole grain bread contains complex carbohydrates and fiber, providing sustained energy compared to simple sugar sources.

quizQuestions.question: Excess carbohydrates consumed beyond energy needs are:
quizQuestions.explanation: When glycogen stores are full, excess dietary carbohydrates are converted to fat and stored in adipose tissue.

quizQuestions.question: A complete protein contains:
options: Only essential amino acids from plants
All nine essential amino acids in adequate amounts
quizQuestions.explanation: A complete protein provides all nine essential amino acids that the body cannot synthesize on its own.

quizQuestions.question: Which food source provides complete protein?
quizQuestions.explanation: Eggs are a complete protein source containing all nine essential amino acids in adequate proportions.

quizQuestions.question: The recommended daily protein intake for active teenagers is approximately:
quizQuestions.explanation: Active adolescents generally need 0.8–1.2 g/kg of body weight daily, with higher amounts for strength athletes.

quizQuestions.question: Protein's primary function in the body is:
options: Building and repairing tissues, enzymes, and hormones
quizQuestions.explanation: Protein provides amino acids for building and repairing muscles, synthesizing enzymes, hormones, antibodies, and structural proteins.

quizQuestions.question: Vegetarians can obtain all essential amino acids by:
options: Combining complementary plant proteins (rice and beans)
quizQuestions.explanation: Combining complementary plant proteins (e.g., rice and beans) provides all essential amino acids that individual plant foods may lack.

quizQuestions.question: Saturated fats are primarily found in:
quizQuestions.explanation: Saturated fats are abundant in animal products like butter, red meat, and full-fat dairy, and are linked to elevated LDL cholesterol.

quizQuestions.question: Trans fats are most harmful because they:
options: Raise LDL (bad) cholesterol and lower HDL (good) cholesterol simultaneously
quizQuestions.explanation: Trans fats raise LDL and lower HDL cholesterol, significantly increasing the risk of cardiovascular disease.

quizQuestions.question: Omega-3 fatty acids are especially beneficial for:
options: Heart health, brain function, and reducing inflammation
quizQuestions.explanation: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA, DHA) reduce inflammation, support cardiovascular health, and promote brain development and function.

quizQuestions.question: Which fat is considered the healthiest choice?
options: Trans fat from partially hydrogenated oils
Saturated fat from coconut oil in excess
Any fat found in fast food
quizQuestions.explanation: Monounsaturated fats, as found in olive oil, avocados, and nuts, are associated with improved heart health and reduced inflammation.

quizQuestions.question: Fat is essential in the diet because it:
options: Is absorbed only by the kidneys
Transports fat-soluble vitamins and supports cell membrane function
quizQuestions.explanation: Fat is essential for absorbing vitamins A, D, E, and K, maintaining cell membrane integrity, hormone production, and providing essential fatty acids.

quizQuestions.question: Which vitamins are fat-soluble?
options: Vitamins A, D, E, and K
quizQuestions.explanation: Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K) are stored in body fat and require dietary fat for absorption; B vitamins and C are water-soluble.

quizQuestions.question: Vitamin C deficiency causes which disease?
quizQuestions.explanation: Scurvy results from vitamin C deficiency, causing collagen breakdown, bleeding gums, poor wound healing, and fatigue.

quizQuestions.question: Vitamin D is unique among vitamins because:
options: It is found only in vegetables
The body can synthesize it from sunlight exposure
It is water-soluble and excreted daily
quizQuestions.explanation: Vitamin D is synthesized in the skin upon sunlight (UV) exposure, making it the only vitamin the body can produce endogenously.

quizQuestions.question: B vitamins primarily function as:
quizQuestions.explanation: B vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate, etc.) act as coenzymes in metabolic reactions that convert food into usable energy.

quizQuestions.question: Vitamin A is essential for which body function?
options: Vision, immune function, and cell growth
quizQuestions.explanation: Vitamin A supports vision (especially night vision), immune defense, skin integrity, and cell growth and differentiation.

quizQuestions.question: Calcium is most critical for which body function?
options: Bone and teeth formation and strength
quizQuestions.explanation: Calcium is the primary mineral in bones and teeth, essential for their formation, density, and structural strength.

quizQuestions.question: Iron deficiency leads to which condition?
quizQuestions.explanation: Iron deficiency reduces hemoglobin production, impairing oxygen delivery and causing iron-deficiency anemia.

quizQuestions.question: Which food is the richest dietary source of calcium?
options: Dairy products like milk and yogurt
quizQuestions.explanation: Dairy products (milk, yogurt, cheese) are among the richest and most bioavailable sources of dietary calcium.

quizQuestions.question: Sodium's primary role in the body is:
options: Regulating fluid balance and blood pressure
quizQuestions.explanation: Sodium is the primary extracellular electrolyte, regulating fluid balance, blood pressure, and nerve signal transmission.

quizQuestions.question: Excessive sodium intake is associated with:
options: Increased risk of high blood pressure and heart disease
quizQuestions.explanation: High sodium intake increases blood pressure (hypertension), a major risk factor for heart disease and stroke.

quizQuestions.question: Water makes up approximately what percentage of the human body?
quizQuestions.explanation: Water makes up approximately 60–70% of the adult body, with higher percentages in lean mass and lower in fat mass.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is an early sign of dehydration?
quizQuestions.explanation: Dark urine and thirst are early indicators of mild dehydration before significant performance or health impairment occurs.

quizQuestions.question: How much additional fluid should an athlete consume per hour of intense exercise?
quizQuestions.explanation: Athletes typically lose 500–1000+ mL of sweat per hour and should replace these losses to maintain performance and safety.

quizQuestions.question: Overhydration (hyponatremia) is caused by:
options: Drinking excessive plain water without adequate electrolytes
quizQuestions.explanation: Hyponatremia occurs when excessive plain water intake dilutes blood sodium levels, causing dangerous electrolyte imbalance.

quizQuestions.question: Which beverage is the best choice for everyday hydration?
quizQuestions.explanation: Water is the ideal everyday hydration choice — it is calorie-free, inexpensive, and perfectly suited for the body's hydration needs.

quizQuestions.question: Energy balance means:
quizQuestions.explanation: Energy balance occurs when caloric intake equals caloric expenditure, resulting in weight maintenance.

quizQuestions.question: Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) refers to:
options: Calories burned at rest to maintain basic body functions
The rate at which food is digested
quizQuestions.explanation: BMR is the number of calories the body burns at rest to maintain basic physiological functions like breathing and circulation.

quizQuestions.question: A caloric deficit of approximately 500 calories per day leads to approximately how much weight loss per week?
quizQuestions.explanation: A 500-calorie daily deficit (3,500 calories per week) theoretically results in approximately 1 pound of fat loss per week.

quizQuestions.question: Crash diets are ineffective for long-term weight management because they:
options: Lead to muscle loss, nutrient deficiencies, and metabolic slowdown
quizQuestions.explanation: Crash diets cause rapid loss of muscle mass, deprive the body of essential nutrients, and trigger metabolic adaptation that slows weight loss.

quizQuestions.question: The most sustainable approach to healthy weight management combines:
options: Balanced nutrition, regular physical activity, and behavioral changes
Only cardio exercise without diet changes
quizQuestions.explanation: Sustainable weight management requires a combination of balanced diet, regular exercise, and healthy behavioral habits maintained over time.

quizQuestions.question: The 2020–2025 Dietary Guidelines for Americans recommend that added sugars contribute no more than:
quizQuestions.explanation: The Dietary Guidelines recommend limiting added sugar to less than 10% of total daily caloric intake.

quizQuestions.question: The Mediterranean diet is characterized by high intake of:
options: Fruits, vegetables, whole grains, legumes, and olive oil
quizQuestions.explanation: The Mediterranean diet emphasizes plant foods, whole grains, healthy fats (olive oil), fish, and limited red meat.

quizQuestions.question: The DASH diet was specifically designed to help manage:
quizQuestions.explanation: The DASH (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) diet was developed to lower blood pressure through diet modification.

quizQuestions.question: A plant-based diet is associated with reduced risk of:
options: Heart disease, type 2 diabetes, and certain cancers
quizQuestions.explanation: Well-planned plant-based diets are associated with reduced risk of chronic diseases including heart disease, diabetes, and certain cancers.

quizQuestions.question: Half of MyPlate should be filled with:
quizQuestions.explanation: The USDA MyPlate recommends filling half your plate with fruits and vegetables at each meal.

quizQuestions.question: The best pre-workout meal should be consumed approximately how long before exercise?
quizQuestions.explanation: Consuming a mixed meal 1–3 hours before exercise allows adequate digestion and provides available energy without GI distress.

quizQuestions.question: Which nutrient is the most important to consume immediately after intense exercise?
quizQuestions.explanation: Post-exercise consumption of both protein (muscle repair) and carbohydrates (glycogen replenishment) optimizes recovery.

quizQuestions.question: Sports drinks are most appropriate for exercise lasting:
options: Longer than 60–90 minutes at moderate-to-high intensity
quizQuestions.explanation: Sports drinks provide electrolytes and carbohydrates that benefit performance during prolonged (60–90+ minutes) moderate-to-high intensity exercise.

quizQuestions.question: Protein supplements (shakes) are necessary for most high school athletes:
options: Yes — all athletes need them for muscle building
No — most athletes can meet protein needs through whole food sources
quizQuestions.explanation: Most athletes can meet protein needs through whole foods; supplements may be convenient but are not necessary for high school athletes.

quizQuestions.question: Carbohydrate loading is most beneficial for athletes competing in:
options: Events lasting 90+ minutes like marathons or soccer
quizQuestions.explanation: Carbohydrate loading maximizes glycogen stores to delay fatigue, benefiting prolonged endurance events of 90+ minutes.

quizQuestions.question: The serving size on a nutrition facts label refers to:
options: The amount per serving suggested by the manufacturer
quizQuestions.explanation: Serving size on a nutrition label reflects the standardized amount per serving and is the basis for all nutritional information listed.

quizQuestions.question: A food labeled 'natural' must legally:
options: Contain no artificial colors or flavors
Have very few specific requirements — it is not tightly regulated
quizQuestions.explanation: The FDA does not have a strict legal definition for 'natural,' making it one of the most misleading marketing claims on food labels.

quizQuestions.question: The % Daily Value (%DV) on a nutrition label is based on a diet of how many calories?
quizQuestions.explanation: The %DV on nutrition labels is calculated based on a reference diet of 2,000 calories per day.

quizQuestions.question: Ingredients on a food label are listed in order of:
options: Weight from most to least (predominant ingredient first)
Caloric content from highest to lowest
quizQuestions.explanation: Ingredients are listed by weight, with the most abundant ingredient listed first, helping consumers assess the food's primary components.

quizQuestions.question: A %DV of 20% or more for sodium indicates:
quizQuestions.explanation: A %DV of 20% or more is considered HIGH for any nutrient; for sodium, this indicates a high-sodium food that should be limited.

quizQuestions.question: Anorexia nervosa is characterized by:
options: Severely restricting food intake due to intense fear of weight gain
quizQuestions.explanation: Anorexia nervosa involves extreme food restriction driven by intense fear of gaining weight and distorted body image.

quizQuestions.question: Bulimia nervosa is characterized by:
options: Repeated binge-eating episodes followed by purging behaviors
quizQuestions.explanation: Bulimia involves cycles of binge eating followed by compensatory behaviors like purging (vomiting, laxatives) or excessive exercise.

quizQuestions.question: Binge Eating Disorder (BED) is different from bulimia because:
options: BED involves recurrent binge episodes WITHOUT compensatory purging
quizQuestions.explanation: BED involves recurrent binge eating episodes accompanied by feelings of loss of control, without the purging behaviors seen in bulimia.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a warning sign of an eating disorder in a peer?
options: Sudden dramatic weight changes, avoiding meals, excessive exercise fixation
quizQuestions.explanation: Warning signs include dramatic weight changes, food avoidance at social events, excessive exercise preoccupation, and distorted body image.

quizQuestions.question: The best first step if you are concerned about a friend with an eating disorder is:
options: Monitor their food intake without telling them
Tell them they look fine and shouldn't worry
Express caring concern privately and encourage them to seek professional help
Post about it on social media
quizQuestions.explanation: Expressing genuine, non-judgmental concern privately and encouraging professional help is the most supportive and effective first step.

quizQuestions.question: During adolescence, increased calcium intake is important primarily for:
quizQuestions.explanation: Adolescence is a critical window for building peak bone mass; adequate calcium and vitamin D intake during this period reduces osteoporosis risk.

quizQuestions.question: Iron needs are higher for adolescent girls than boys primarily because:
quizQuestions.explanation: Menstrual blood loss increases iron needs in adolescent girls; inadequate intake can lead to iron-deficiency anemia.

quizQuestions.question: Older adults often need more of which nutrient to support bone health?
quizQuestions.explanation: Older adults need higher calcium and vitamin D intake to compensate for decreased absorption efficiency and bone density loss with aging.

quizQuestions.question: Pregnancy increases the need for which nutrient to prevent neural tube defects?
quizQuestions.explanation: Folate (folic acid) is critical early in pregnancy to prevent neural tube defects; pregnant women need at least 600 mcg daily.

quizQuestions.question: Caloric needs generally decrease in older adulthood because:
options: Basal metabolic rate decreases with reduced muscle mass
Older adults exercise more than young adults
quizQuestions.explanation: Aging is associated with reduced muscle mass (sarcopenia) and decreased physical activity, lowering overall caloric requirements.

quizQuestions.question: Which sector of the food system is responsible for producing raw agricultural products?
quizQuestions.explanation: Food production refers to farming and agriculture — growing crops, raising livestock, and producing the raw materials of the food supply.

quizQuestions.question: Food waste contributes to environmental problems primarily by:
options: Generating greenhouse gas emissions in landfills and wasting resources used in production
quizQuestions.explanation: Food decomposing in landfills produces methane (a potent greenhouse gas), while all the water, land, and energy used to produce wasted food is also lost.

quizQuestions.question: Which dietary choice generally has the smallest environmental footprint?
options: High consumption of beef and lamb
Daily consumption of highly processed foods
quizQuestions.explanation: Plant-based foods generally require less land, water, and energy to produce and generate fewer greenhouse gas emissions than animal-based foods.

quizQuestions.question: Food insecurity means:
options: Lack of consistent access to enough food for an active, healthy life
Poor food safety practices at home
quizQuestions.explanation: Food insecurity is the state of being without reliable access to sufficient amounts of affordable, nutritious food.

quizQuestions.question: Choosing locally grown, seasonal foods is a sustainable practice because it:
options: Reduces transportation emissions and supports local economies
Has no effect on food quality
Is only possible in summer months
quizQuestions.explanation: Local, seasonal foods reduce the carbon footprint of transportation, support regional farmers, and are often fresher and more nutritious.

questions.question: Which of the six nutrient classes provides 9 calories per gram?
questions.explanation: Fat provides 9 calories per gram — more than twice the energy density of carbohydrates or protein (4 cal/g each).

questions.question: The glycemic index of a food indicates:
options: How rapidly it raises blood glucose levels
questions.explanation: The glycemic index measures how quickly a food raises blood sugar; high-GI foods cause rapid spikes.

questions.question: Complete proteins contain:
options: All nine essential amino acids in adequate amounts
questions.explanation: Complete proteins supply all nine essential amino acids the body cannot make on its own.

questions.question: Trans fats are especially harmful because they:
options: Raise LDL and lower HDL cholesterol simultaneously
questions.explanation: Trans fats have a dual negative effect: raising LDL (bad) cholesterol while lowering HDL (good) cholesterol, increasing heart disease risk.

questions.question: Which vitamins are fat-soluble?
questions.explanation: Vitamins A, D, E, and K are fat-soluble, requiring dietary fat for absorption and stored in body fat.

questions.question: Iron deficiency primarily causes:
questions.explanation: Iron deficiency reduces hemoglobin production, resulting in iron-deficiency anemia with fatigue, pallor, and reduced oxygen transport.

questions.question: Which is an early sign of dehydration?
questions.explanation: Dark yellow urine and thirst are among the earliest detectable signs of mild dehydration.

questions.question: Energy balance occurs when:
options: Calories consumed are less than calories burned
questions.explanation: Energy balance means caloric intake equals caloric expenditure, resulting in weight maintenance over time.

questions.question: The 2020–2025 Dietary Guidelines limit added sugars to less than:
questions.explanation: The Dietary Guidelines for Americans recommend consuming less than 10% of daily calories from added sugars.

questions.question: Sports drinks are most appropriate for exercise lasting:
questions.explanation: Sports drinks with electrolytes and carbohydrates become beneficial during sustained moderate-to-high intensity exercise lasting 60–90+ minutes.

questions.question: Omega-3 fatty acids are primarily known for supporting:
options: Heart health, brain function, and reducing inflammation
questions.explanation: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) reduce inflammation and support cardiovascular health and brain function.

questions.question: Which food contains a complete protein?
questions.explanation: Eggs are a complete protein containing all nine essential amino acids in adequate amounts.

questions.question: Dietary fiber's primary health benefit is:
options: Supporting digestive health, satiety, and blood sugar regulation
questions.explanation: Dietary fiber promotes healthy digestion, reduces cholesterol absorption, supports blood sugar control, and increases satiety.

questions.question: The recommended daily fluid intake for active teenagers is approximately:
options: 2–3+ liters depending on activity and climate
questions.explanation: Active teenagers generally need 2–3+ liters of fluid daily, with additional amounts needed during exercise and in hot weather.

questions.question: Calcium is most important during adolescence for:
options: Building peak bone mass for lifelong bone health
questions.explanation: Adolescence is the critical window for peak bone mass development; adequate calcium intake during this period reduces lifelong osteoporosis risk.

questions.question: Which MyPlate recommendation helps most with chronic disease prevention?
options: Fill half your plate with grains
Fill half your plate with fruits and vegetables
Prioritize protein over all other groups
questions.explanation: The MyPlate recommendation to fill half the plate with fruits and vegetables is most strongly associated with chronic disease prevention.

questions.question: Anorexia nervosa is primarily characterized by:
options: Severe food restriction and intense fear of weight gain
questions.explanation: Anorexia involves extreme caloric restriction, distorted body image, and intense fear of gaining weight.

questions.question: Older adults' caloric needs decrease with age primarily because:
options: Basal metabolic rate declines with reduced muscle mass
questions.explanation: Age-related muscle loss (sarcopenia) and reduced physical activity lower overall energy requirements in older adults.

questions.question: Food waste in landfills contributes to climate change by producing:
questions.explanation: Organic food decomposing in landfills produces methane, a greenhouse gas approximately 25 times more potent than CO2 over a century.

questions.question: The DASH diet was designed to reduce:
questions.explanation: The DASH diet (Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension) was specifically developed to lower elevated blood pressure.

questions.question: Which nutrient is uniquely produced by the body through sunlight exposure?
questions.explanation: Vitamin D is synthesized in the skin when exposed to ultraviolet B (UVB) radiation from sunlight.

questions.question: Post-workout nutrition should emphasize which two nutrients?
questions.explanation: Post-exercise recovery is optimized by consuming protein for muscle repair and carbohydrates for glycogen replenishment.

questions.question: Which statement best describes food insecurity?
options: Lack of consistent access to sufficient, affordable, nutritious food
questions.explanation: Food insecurity refers to the limited or uncertain availability of nutritionally adequate and safe food.

questions.question: The percent Daily Value (%DV) on a food label is based on a daily intake of:
questions.explanation: All %DV calculations on nutrition labels are based on a reference diet of 2,000 calories per day.

questions.question: Carbohydrate loading benefits athletes competing in events lasting:
questions.explanation: Carbohydrate loading maximizes muscle glycogen stores, delaying fatigue in endurance events lasting 90 minutes or more.

questions.question: Folate (folic acid) is especially important during pregnancy to prevent:
questions.explanation: Folate is essential for proper neural tube development in early pregnancy; deficiency is linked to spina bifida and anencephaly.

questions.question: Ingredients on a food label are listed in order of:
options: Weight from most to least abundant
questions.explanation: Food ingredients are listed by weight from the most to least abundant ingredient in the product.

questions.question: Choosing locally grown, seasonal produce supports sustainability by:
options: Reducing transportation emissions and supporting local farmers
questions.explanation: Local seasonal foods reduce the carbon footprint from transportation and storage while supporting regional agricultural economies.

questions.question: Binge Eating Disorder differs from bulimia because BED:
options: Involves binges without compensatory purging behaviors
questions.explanation: BED involves recurrent binge eating episodes without purging behaviors, distinguishing it from bulimia nervosa.

questions.question: The Mediterranean diet's heart health benefits are largely attributed to:
options: High saturated fat from red meat
High fruit, vegetable, whole grain, legume, and olive oil intake
High sugar and processed food consumption
questions.explanation: The Mediterranean diet's cardiovascular benefits come from its high intake of anti-inflammatory plant foods and heart-healthy fats like olive oil.

questions.question: Which macronutrient has the highest caloric density?
questions.explanation: Fat provides 9 calories per gram, the highest energy density of any macronutrient.

questions.question: Vitamin C deficiency leads to:
questions.explanation: Scurvy is caused by vitamin C deficiency, leading to collagen breakdown, bleeding gums, and impaired wound healing.

questions.question: Which dietary pattern has the smallest environmental carbon footprint?
questions.explanation: Plant-based foods require significantly less land, water, and energy and produce fewer greenhouse gas emissions than animal products.

questions.question: Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) represents:
options: Calories burned at rest to maintain vital body functions
Total daily energy needs including all activity
Caloric intake from a typical meal
questions.explanation: BMR is the minimum energy the body needs at complete rest to sustain vital functions like breathing, circulation, and thermoregulation.

questions.question: Sodium's excessive intake is most associated with increased risk of:
options: High blood pressure and cardiovascular disease
questions.explanation: Excessive sodium intake elevates blood pressure (hypertension), a major risk factor for heart disease, stroke, and kidney disease.
